content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 13, 1994


Marjorie Gelb
Assistant City Attorney
City of Berkeley
2180 Milvia Street
Berkeley, CA 94804

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-93-340

Dear Ms. Gelb:

This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
the City of Berkeley's public access corporation concerning their
duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act.1/ Since your request for advice does not refer to a
specific pending governmental decision, we are treating your
request as one for informal assistance.2/

Please note that the Commission does not act as the finder of
fact in providing advice. Our letters are based on the facts
presented to us. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Does the City of Berkeley's public access corporation meet
the third criterion in the Commission's opinion in In re Siegel
(1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 62, that is, is it providing services or
undertaking obligations which public agencies are legally
authorized to perform and which, in fact, they traditionally have
performed?


1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Advice File No. I-93-340
Page 3


considered public cable access corporations in its Cormier Advice
Letter, No. A-84-202. The Commission concluded that the public
access corporation was a local government agency.

It is your contention that the City of Berkeley's cable
corporation is comparatively different from the cable corporation
cited in the Cormier letter and thus should not be deemed to meet
the third criterion of Siegel.

In In re Siegel, the Commission believed several criteria
should be considered when analyzing whether an entity is a local
government agency within the meaning of the Act. You have
specifically asked about the third criterion:

(3) Whether one of the principal purposes for which
it is formed is to provide services or undertake
obligations which public agencies are legally authorized
to perform and which, in fact, they traditionally have
performed.

Thus, the issue is not whether the entity holds public
meetings or files reports but whether the corporation "provides
services or undertakes obligations which public agencies are
legally authorized to perform and which in fact have traditionally
performed."

In Cormier we said:

"The purpose of the Corporation is one which the
City is legally authorized to perform under the laws
concerning community antenna television systems.
(Government Code Sections 53066-53077; see specifically
Section 53066.1(j).) While the type of services the
Corporation is performing are not necessarily
traditional of public agencies since cable television
systems are a relatively recent phenomenon, it is true
that both the law and many other cities view the role of
providing community access to cable television and
monitoring the operations of the system as a public
function. Based on all of these factors, the
Corporation is essentially a entity created by the City
to accomplish governmental purpose and is therefore a
'local government agency' within the meaning of Section
82041."

It is also important to note that historically, regulation of
other forms of media has been a governmental function. (See e.g.
Syracuse Peace Council v. FCC (1989) 867 F.2d 654.)

Thus, consistent with Cormier, the role of providing
community access to cable television and monitoring the operations
of the system is a public function. Thus, we see no reason to
change the conclusion. The corporation was formed for the

Advice File No. I-93-340
Page 2


CONCLUSION

The public access corporation is performing a function which
public agencies are legally authorized to perform. It thus meets
the third criterion of the Siegel Opinion and consistent with our
previous advice, the corporation would be a public agency.

DISCUSSION

Your letter requests we reconsider advice previously given to
you. (Gelb Advice Letter No. I-93-624.) In that letter, we
concluded that the City of Berkeley's public access corporation is
a local government agency subject to the provisions of the Act.

According to your facts, the city of Berkeley created a cable
task force to help the city perform its cable assessment and to
negotiate its cable franchise renewal with the cable company.
Pursuant to the recommendations of the cable task force, the city
negotiated for a designation of public, education and government
access stations and a substantial capital commitment for equipment
in order to allow the public, the government and schools to
produce and broadcast cable programs.

One of the recommendations by the task force was the creation
of a nonprofit public access corporation ("corporation") to
administer the public, education and government cable stations,
coordinate the production and broadcast of programming of private
citizens and the government, and teach the public how to use the
equipment.

In your original November 12, 1992 request for advice you
indicated that the impetus for formation of the corporation
originated with the city. The first criterion of Siegel was met.
You also stated that it was clearly intended that the city will
fund the corporation. Thus, the second criterion in the Siegel
Opinion had been met. However, no information was provided to
conclude that the corporation was treated as a government agency
by other statutes, thus it appeared that the fourth criterion in
Siegel had not been met. There are no new facts with respect to
these criterion and they are not the subject of your request for
reconsideration.

Your request for reconsideration pivots on the third
criterion of Siegel, i.e., whether the entity is providing a
service which public agencies are legally authorized to perform.
In our letter to you, we stated that the Commission had previously

Advice File No. I-93-340
Page 4


principal purpose of providing a service or otherwise taking on an
obligation which the city is legally authorized to perform.
Therefore, we reaffirm our conclusion on this point.

I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any additional questions, please feel free
to contact the Commission's Legal Division at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

SC/JET/jt
